raw_chunk_text:
We just don’t want to just survive uncertainty, to just about make it. We want to
survive uncertainty and, in addition—like a certain class of aggressive Roman Stoics—
have the last word. The mission
rule_text: A trader should construct positions that gain from volatility and disorder rather than merely protect against them, positioning to benefit from shocks and stressors.
entry_conditions: Realized volatility exceeds 90th percentile of 252-day rolling history
VIX or equivalent crosses 75th percentile threshold
Market exhibits observable disorder (gap moves, limit moves, or high intraday ranges)
exit_conditions: Realized volatility falls below 60th percentile
Position P&L reaches +50% of initial risk capital
Calendar exit after 20 trading days

rule_text: Build positions and strategies that are explicitly designed to benefit from uncertainty and volatility rather than attempting to predict specific outcomes; focus on identifying fragility in systems and reducing it before seeking gains.
entry_conditions: Portfolio exhibits measurable fragility (high drawdown sensitivity to unexpected shocks)
Implied volatility regime below 20th percentile (market complacent)
Position sizing: allocate 1-2% of portfolio to antifragile instruments
exit_conditions: Portfolio fragility measure decreases below threshold
Cumulative P&L on antifragile sleeve reaches +3% of allocation

raw_chunk_text:
In every domain or area of application, we propose rules for moving from the fragile
toward the antifragile, through reduction of fragility or harnessing antifragility. And we

rule_text: A trader should seek positions with asymmetric payoff structures (more upside than downside from random shocks) and avoid suppressing volatility exposure, as systems deprived of stressors and randomness become fragile and eventually fail.
entry_conditions: Upside_potential / Downside_risk >= 3.0
Current_portfolio_volatility_suppression_ratio <= 0.2
Position exists in at least one unhedged volatile asset class
exit_conditions: Asymmetry ratio falls below 2.5
Volatility suppression exceeds 0.3
Unhedged exposure drops below 10%

raw_chunk_text:
CHAPTER 3
The Cat and the Washing Machine
Stress is knowledge (and knowledge is stress)—The organic and the
mechanical—No translator needed, for now—Waking up the animal in us,
after two hundred years
rule_text: A trader should expose their portfolio to episodic, bounded stressors rather than chronic low-level stress, because biological systems (and by extension, trading systems) strengthen and adapt through recovery from discrete shocks within a tolerable range.
entry_conditions: Portfolio drawdown from peak < 3%
Time since last stress event > 60 trading days
Volatility regime is NORMAL to HIGH (not crisis)
Initiate a small directional position opposite recent trend
exit_conditions: Exit when loss reaches 1.5% OR 5 days elapsed
Do not re-stress until 60 days have passed

rule_text: A trader should expose themselves to frequent acute stressors followed by adequate recovery periods, rather than chronic low-level stress, to build antifragility and allow stress to function as a messenger for improvement.

Position size: 2-3% of account per trade
Exit position within 1-2 trading sessions
Enforce 5-7 consecutive days of zero trading after exit
exit_conditions: Exit after 1-2 days regardless of profit/loss
Stop-loss at 3% account drawdown
Mandatory exit at day boundary if still open

rule_text: Learning and skill acquisition occur best through real-world stress and error correction under necessity, not through theoretical study in isolation. Trader limits live exposure to 1-2 trades per week while maintaining paper trading
exit_conditions: Exit when 3 consecutive losses occur
Exit and return to pure theory if capital drawdown reaches 5%

Recall our mission to “not be a turkey.” The take-home is that, when facing a long
sample subjected to turkey problems, one tends to estimate a lower number of adverse
events—simply, rare events are r
rule_text: A trader should adjust position sizing and risk tolerance upward when facing positive asymmetries (antifragile payoff structures) and downward when facing negative asymmetries (fragile payoff structures), because historical samples systematically underestimate hidden risks in fragile cases and hidden opportunities in antifragile cases.
entry_conditions: Identify payoff asymmetry via option pricing or skew analysis
Classify position as fragile (negative skew, tail risk) or antifragile (positive skew, tail benefit)
Estimate hidden event probability using 2x historical sample frequency for fragile cases
exit_conditions: Exit fragile positions if realized loss exceeds 8% of capital
Trim antifragile positions if skewness collapses below 0.1

rule_text: A trader should apply episodic deprivation (stressor variability) to portfolio management rather than smooth, continuous exposure, because nonlinear physiological responses to stressors activate beneficial adaptation pathways that produce better long-term outcomes than consistent steady-state approaches.
entry_conditions: Position held continuously for 20+ trading days
No market regime shift detected
exit_conditions: Complete exit after 16 days of full exposure
Return to position after 4 days of zero exposure

Evidence exists that consensus is dangerously wrong
Confidence level ≥70% based on structural analysis
Position sizing ≤10% of portfolio per thesis; allow leverage up to 1.5x
exit_conditions: Thesis disproven
Position reaches 50% drawdown
Timeline exceeded by 2x initial estimate

rule_text: Detect fragility in a system by measuring the acceleration (second derivative) of deterioration: when incremental stress produces disproportionately larger losses, the system is overoptimized and exposure should be reduced until the response curve flattens.
entry_conditions: Existing open position in market
Measure loss over stress period T
Measure loss over next equal stress period T: ΔL₂/ΔL₁ ≥ 1.5
exit_conditions: Exit entire position when ΔL₂/ΔL₁ ≥ 1.5
Or rebalance when second derivative of unrealized loss > 0

rule_text: A trader should limit position size and transaction scale when operating in systems with negative convexity (one-sided downside errors), because errors in fragile systems compound negatively whereas errors in robust systems wash out.
entry_conditions: Instrument identified as structurally fragile (e.g., illiquid, tail-risk-prone, event-driven)
Position size set at maximum 1% account risk per trade
Individual transaction size capped at 0.5% of portfolio

rule_text: A trader should limit position size and transaction scale when operating in systems with negative convexity (one-sided downside errors), because errors in fragile systems compound negatively whereas errors in robust systems wash out.
entry_conditions: Classify instrument: robust (symmetric errors) vs fragile (negative convexity)
For robust instruments: position size up to 3% account risk
For fragile instruments: position size capped at 1.5% account risk
exit_conditions: Tight stops on fragile systems (0.75% max loss)
Normal stops on robust systems (1.5% max loss)
Rebalance classification weekly based on error distribution

rule_text: A trader should limit position size and transaction scale when operating in systems with negative convexity (one-sided downside errors), because errors in fragile systems compound negatively whereas errors in robust systems wash out.
entry_conditions: Measure realized error asymmetry ratio: (downside errors) / (upside errors) over trailing 30 trades
If ratio < 1.2 (near-symmetric): allow position size up to 5% account risk
If ratio 1.2–1.8 (moderate asymmetry): cap at 2.5% account risk
If ratio > 1.8 (high negative convexity): cap at 1% account risk
exit_conditions: Exits follow position size tier: tight stops for high-asymmetry, relaxed for symmetric
Recalculate asymmetry ratio weekly; adjust sizing tier immediately if threshold crossed

raw_chunk_text:
CHAPTER 20
Time and Fragility
Prophecy, like knowledge, is subtractive, not additive—The Lindy effect,
or how the old prevails over the new, especially in technology, no matter
what they say in Califo
rule_text: Prefer established (old) assets and strategies that have survived time over new ones, because time reveals hidden fragilities; what survives has proven utility.
entry_conditions: Asset has survived >50 years of continuous trading
Strategy has documented performance >30 years
Asset shows stable bid-ask spreads across market cycles
No structural changes to underlying mechanism in past 20 years
exit_conditions: Asset undergoes structural change or merger
Strategy begins showing deterioration vs historical baseline
Liquidity dries up

rule_text: Distinguish between Black Swan–prone predictions (which become more reliable over longer horizons as tail events materialize) and typical predictions (which degrade in accuracy with time due to nonlinearities), and allocate conviction and position sizing accordingly.
entry_conditions: Identify asset or system as Black Swan–prone (fragile to tail events)
Minimum holding period >= 10 years confirmed
OR explicitly avoid typical forecasts with horizon > 1 year
exit_conditions: Exit typical predictions at 12-month mark regardless of P&L
Hold Black Swan positions until 10+ year horizon or fragility resolved

rule_text: Apply risk-taking proportionally to problem severity: minimize intervention in marginal/healthy cases but increase risk exposure substantially in severe/critical cases, following a convex (nonlinear) response function.

exit_conditions: Severity indicator falls below 40th percentile
Position reaches 4% loss threshold

exit_conditions: Do not enter; trade rejected at screening stage

powerful study by Emre Soyer and Robin Hogarth showed that many professionals and

rule_text: A trader should bias toward inaction and skepticism of quantitative estimates rather than act on expert consensus that underestimates randomness and uncertainty, because professionals systematically mistranslate statistical models into reality.

Profit target or 20% loss, whichever first

In other words, the response for, say, 50 percent of a certain dose during one period, followed by 150 percent of
the dose in a subsequent period in convex cases, is superior to 100 percent of the dos
rule_text: Vary exposure intensity across periods rather than maintaining constant exposure; convex payoff structures benefit from unequal dose distribution (high-low or low-high sequencing) more than equal allocation.

Market regime identified as non-trending or mean-reverting
Capital allocation cycle allows for periodic sizing adjustment
exit_conditions: Exit when position loses convexity characteristics

Rebalance if sizing variance drifts beyond ±5% target

rule_text: Introduce deliberate irregularity and randomness into portfolio composition and trade frequency to exploit nonlinear payoff structures; avoid mechanical consistency in allocation and entry timing.

exit_conditions: Exit at predetermined profit target or stop loss (mechanical exit rules unchanged)
Rebalance timing randomized but not exit discipline

raw_chunk_text:
Fat Tony has two heuristics.
First, never get on a plane if the pilot is not on board.
Second, make sure there is also a copilot.
The first heuristic addresses the asymmetry in rewards and punishment,
rule_text: Only participate in decisions, strategies, or recommendations when the decision-maker bears proportional personal risk (skin in the game) from the consequences of that decision.
entry_conditions: Advisor/manager publicly discloses personal holdings matching recommended position
Advisor's personal capital at risk equals or exceeds AUM percentage committed to same trade
Advisor's personal drawdown experience published and transparent
Verify advisor personally holds position for minimum 90 days before recommending
exit_conditions: Advisor reduces personal position below entry threshold
Advisor stops publicly reporting personal holdings
Advisor separates from personal capital in trade

Fundamental Asymmetry (also Seneca’s Asymmetry): When someone has more
upside than downside in a certain situation, he is antifragile and tends to gain from (a)
volatility, (b) randomness, (c) errors,
rule_text: A trader should construct positions with asymmetric payoff structures where potential gains exceed potential losses, enabling the portfolio to benefit from volatility, randomness, and time rather than suffer from them.
entry_conditions: Identified situation with asymmetric payoff structure
Upside/Downside ratio >= 3.0
Position size <= 2% of portfolio per trade
Limited downside clearly defined (e.g., option premium paid)
exit_conditions: Asymmetry collapses below 2.0
Time decay erodes position beyond 50% of entry
Maximum loss of 2% portfolio hit

rule_text: A trader should combine a safe, stable position with speculative exposure to optionality, rather than deploying capital in a single modal strategy, to achieve antifragility and benefit from tail events.
entry_conditions: Establish core stable position (bonds, cash, dividend stocks, or short vol strategies)
Allocate remaining capital to out-of-the-money options or speculative vehicles
exit_conditions: Exit speculative positions at predetermined profit target (50-100% return)
Hold safe positions indefinitely or rebalance quarterly

rule_text: A trader should engage in small, controlled trial-and-error experiments with limited downside rather than avoid action entirely, because small errors build resilience and prepare the system for larger exposures.

Position size set to risked capital ≤ 0.25% of account
Hard stop-loss placed at entry price ± 1 ATR (or 0.5% max loss)
exit_conditions: Exit immediately at hard stop-loss
Exit at 1.5:1 RR achieved
Exit after 5 bars if no directional conviction

rule_text: Focus on tracking a single dominant variable that naturally leads to correct positioning, rather than computing all causal factors independently.
entry_conditions: Single tracked variable shows directional change for 3+ consecutive periods
Entry when variable crosses 1.0 standard deviation from 20-period mean
Optional secondary variable confirmation (but not required)
exit_conditions: Exit on mean reversion of tracked variable or loss of directional momentum
Hard stop at 2.0 stddev adverse move

source_citation: Antifragile, Nassim Taleb, Ch.25, pp.435-581 (gaze heuristic section)
raw_chunk_text:

rule_text: Focus on tracking a single dominant variable that naturally leads to correct positioning, rather than computing all causal factors independently.
entry_conditions: Single tracked variable crosses 0.5 standard deviations from 20-period mean
Entry on first bar of directional cross; no additional confirmation required
Trade the variable's momentum without computing underlying drivers
exit_conditions: Exit on reversal of tracked variable or after fixed 10-bar holding period
Hard stop at 1.5 stddev adverse move
